options.pricing: Pay-per-second GPU; CPU from ~$0.000025/s up to ~$0.001525/s (H100). Cold starts on unpopular models add 20-60s of billed GPU time (~$0.02-0.08/run) — pin/warm hot models or use Deployments.
pros: Turns the hard GPU problem into simple HTTP calls (workers stay thin)
Async + webhooks fit the durable-step pattern perfectly
Cog packages custom PBR/upscale models
Cloudflare acquisition aligns it with R2/Workers
User already has a Replicate key
cons: Must copy outputs within ~1h before deletion
Cold-start cost on niche models
Per-second cost variance complicates credit pricing
Acquisition adds some long-term roadmap uncertainty
Verify webhook signatures or risk spoofed completions

options.name: Cloudflare R2 — output/asset storage (RECOMMENDED for the multi-file 4K downloads)
options.what: Store inputs and the multi-file 4K PBR sets + the packaged .zip here. ZERO egress fees mean high-volume large downloads (a 4K ORM+normal+albedo+height zip can be 50-150MB+) don't rack up bandwidth charges. S3-compatible API (drop-in with the AWS SDK / presigned URLs); serve downloads via short-lived presigned URLs fronted by Cloudflare's CDN. Strategically aligned now that Replicate is a Cloudflare company.
options.pricing: Storage ~$0.015/GB-mo; $0 egress (via Workers / S3 API / r2.dev). Free tier available. For download-heavy media ~50-80% cheaper than S3/Supabase egress.
pros: Zero egress = predictable cost as downloads scale
S3-compatible (presigned URLs, standard tooling)
Cloudflare CDN in front
Aligns with the Cloudflare/Replicate direction
Clean split — public deliverables on R2
cons: Separate from Supabase Auth (enforce access by minting short-lived presigned URLs from an authorized API route, not via RLS)
One more credential/bucket to manage
Zip packaging is your code (a Worker or an orchestrator step)

options.name: Supabase Storage — alternative storage (simplest if you want one vendor)
options.what: Keep inputs/outputs inside Supabase so storage objects inherit Auth + RLS (a user only lists/reads their own assets via policies) and you stay single-vendor with the connected MCP. S3-compatible API since 2024, native signed URLs (createSignedUrl). Good default for authenticated USER inputs even if you later move public/large deliverables to R2.
options.pricing: Pro $25/mo includes 100GB storage + 250GB egress; overage $0.021/GB storage, $0.09/GB egress — egress gets expensive on heavy 4K-zip download traffic.
pros: Tightest integration with DB + Auth (RLS-gated access, no separate authz layer)
Native signed URLs
One vendor, one bill, one MCP
Ideal for private user inputs
cons: $0.09/GB egress hurts on large, frequent 4K downloads (the exact workload here)
For a download-centric product this is the costliest axis vs R2's $0 egress

options.name: Supabase Postgres + Auth + Realtime — persistence, identity, job state machine + progress streaming
options.what: The system's backbone (connected MCP). Postgres holds users, credit balances, and a jobs table that IS the pipeline state machine: status (queued/classifying/tiling/deriving/upscaling/packing/done/failed), current_step, progress %, per-step Replicate prediction ids, input/output storage keys, error, idempotency_key. Supabase Auth handles login (RLS scopes every row to its owner). For progress streaming, use Supabase Realtime: the orchestrator/webhook UPDATEs the job row and the browser receives it over a websocket — no polling, no custom SSE server. Prefer Realtime Broadcast over Postgres-Changes at scale (Postgres-Changes does one authz read per subscriber per event).
options.pricing: Free tier to start; Pro $25/mo (includes DB, Auth, Storage, Realtime, Queues, cron). One bill for most of the backend.
pros: One service covers DB + Auth + Realtime + Storage + Queues (huge ops reduction for a solo founder)
Realtime gives live progress with ~no extra infra
RLS enforces per-user isolation
The jobs table doubles as state machine + audit log
MCP already connected for migrations/SQL
cons: Postgres-Changes realtime has known fan-out scaling limits — use Broadcast for busy channels
You still need a separate durable orchestrator (Trigger.dev) OR the pgmq/cron pattern for the long-running execution itself
Storage egress is pricey unless you offload downloads to R2

options.name: Stripe Billing — credits + subscriptions metered to GPU cost
options.what: Monetization. Two-layer model fits AI cost best: (1) sell CREDITS / a subscription with included credits; (2) meter actual consumption with Stripe Meters + Meter Events tied to Replicate's per-second GPU billing. Critical pattern for high per-unit GPU cost: PRE-AUTHORIZE — before starting a job, reserve the estimated credit cost against the user's balance (reject if insufficient); after the pipeline finishes, SETTLE against the real metered seconds. Mirrors card preauth and prevents users running expensive 4K upscales they can't pay for. Aggregate usage app-side and flush one meter event per job (not per model call) to stay under rate limits.
options.pricing: Standard Stripe fees + usage-based billing features; real-time credit burndown and credit-grant automation supported natively in 2026.
pros: Native credit burndown + metered billing + subscriptions in one platform
preauth/settle maps cleanly to per-second GPU cost so you never lose money on a job
Meter aggregation keeps you under rate limits
Well-trodden for AI/SaaS in 2026
cons: Reconciling Stripe meters with Replicate per-second cost needs careful estimation (cold starts inflate actuals — pad reservations)
Usage-based billing adds implementation surface
Webhook-driven balance updates must be idempotent (store Stripe event ids)

recommendation:
CONCRETE STACK for a solo founder (low ops, ship fast, uses the connected MCPs):

1) FRONTEND + THIN API: Next.js 15 App Router on Vercel (deploy via the Vercel MCP). RSC for marketing/dashboard; client components only for the R3F preview and the live progress bar. Route Handlers do exactly three things and return fast: validate the upload, mint a storage URL, create the job row + enqueue. Never run the pipeline in a Vercel function — it will time out (300s Hobby / ~800s Pro).

2) 3D PREVIEW: react-three-fiber + drei + three.js. meshStandardMaterial with map/normalMap/roughnessMap/metalnessMap/aoMap/displacementMap on a sphere+plane, an HDRI Environment for IBL, OrbitControls, and a repeat/RepeatWrapping toggle to prove seamlessness. Preview from the UE5 ORM-packed channels (AO/Rough/Metal = R/G/B) at downscaled 1K-2K; full 4K only on download.

3) ORCHESTRATION (the hard part): Trigger.dev as the durable workflow engine. Model the pipeline as checkpointed steps: classify -> seamless -> derive PBR maps -> upscale 4K -> pack/zip. Tasks have no timeout, idle waits are checkpointed (you don't pay while Replicate runs), and you get retries + idempotency keys + concurrency caps for free. Start on the $10/mo Hobby tier; Apache-2.0 means you can self-host later if cost/lock-in bites. (If you want ZERO extra vendors at launch, the fallback is Supabase Queues/pgmq + pg_cron + an Edge Function worker — same async pattern, more hand-rolled. If you go all-in on Vercel, Vercel Workflows is the native alternative.)

4) GPU: Replicate (user has the key) in ASYNC mode for every model step — POST returns immediately, completion arrives via a signed webhook (filter [\"completed\"], verify the signature). Keep workers thin: Replicate/Higgsfield/Pixa do the heavy lifting; the orchestrator only sequences and updates state. CRITICAL: Replicate deletes outputs ~1h after completion, so the webhook step must immediately copy maps into your storage.

5) STORAGE: split it. Supabase Storage for authenticated USER INPUTS (RLS-gated, single-vendor simplicity). Cloudflare R2 for the multi-file 4K OUTPUTS + the .zip — zero egress is decisive for a download-heavy product and aligns with Cloudflare now owning Replicate. Serve downloads via short-lived presigned R2 URLs behind the Cloudflare CDN.

6) STATE + AUTH + PROGRESS: Supabase Postgres + Auth + Realtime (MCP connected). A jobs table IS the state machine (status, current_step, progress%, per-step prediction ids, storage keys, idempotency_key). Auth + RLS scope every row to its owner. Push progress to the browser via Supabase Realtime over websockets (use Broadcast, not Postgres-Changes, for busy channels) — no custom SSE/polling server needed.

7) BILLING: Stripe credits/subscription + metered usage. PRE-AUTHORIZE estimated credits before a job, SETTLE against real Replicate per-second cost after; flush one aggregated meter event per job. Pad reservations for cold starts.

Why this wins for a solo founder: the only services to operate are Vercel + Supabase (both already wired via MCP) + Trigger.dev + Stripe + Replicate keys — five managed accounts, almost no servers. The timeout problem is solved by Trigger.dev's durable steps, not by fighting serverless limits. Workers stay thin because Replicate carries the GPU load. Costs stay controlled via R2 zero-egress, checkpoint-on-wait (no paying for GPU idle), concurrency caps, and Stripe preauth/settle. Fixed floor is roughly $20 (Vercel Pro) + $25 (Supabase Pro) + $10 (Trigger.dev Hobby) ~= $55/mo before variable GPU/storage, with a free-tier path to validate first.

Lock-in escape hatches if needed: Trigger.dev is open-source/self-hostable; Replicate models packaged with Cog run anywhere Docker runs; R2 is S3-compatible.
keyTakeaways: THE constraint: a 30s-several-minute multi-model pipeline cannot run inside any serverless request — Vercel caps at 300s (Hobby/Fluid) to ~800s (Pro), Supabase Edge Functions at ~150-400s. The whole architecture exists to move that work off the request path into a durable, checkpointed orchestrator.

Winning pattern: thin Vercel API enqueues -> durable orchestrator runs checkpointed steps -> each step fires a Replicate ASYNC prediction (returns instantly, completes via signed webhook) -> step copies outputs to storage, updates the Postgres job row -> client watches via a Supabase Realtime websocket. Replicate does GPU; the orchestrator just sequences + retries; workers stay thin.

Orchestrator pick for a solo founder: Trigger.dev ($10/mo, no task timeout, checkpoint-on-wait so you don't pay during GPU waits, built-in retries/idempotency/concurrency, Apache-2.0 self-host escape). Alternatives: Inngest (better event fan-out but ~$75/mo), Vercel Workflows (native, GA 2026, deepest lock-in), or Supabase Queues+pgmq+cron (zero extra vendor, but you hand-roll retries/observability).

2026 strategic fact: Cloudflare acquired Replicate (closed early 2026). API unchanged for now, but it makes a Cloudflare-aligned stack (Replicate GPU + R2 zero-egress storage + Workers/CDN) coherent. Hard operational gotcha: Replicate deletes prediction outputs ~1 hour after completion — the webhook handler MUST copy the 4K maps to your storage immediately.

Storage split beats single-vendor: Supabase Storage for RLS-gated user INPUTS (simple, auth-integrated) + Cloudflare R2 for the heavy multi-file 4K OUTPUTS/zip (zero egress vs Supabase $0.09/GB and S3 — 50-80% cheaper for download-heavy traffic). Serve via short-lived presigned URLs behind a CDN.

Progress streaming: prefer Supabase Realtime over websockets (orchestrator UPDATEs job row -> browser updates live) instead of building an SSE/polling server. Use Realtime Broadcast rather than Postgres-Changes for busy channels (Postgres-Changes does one authz read per subscriber per event and bottlenecks at scale).

Billing tied to GPU cost: Stripe credits/subscription + metered usage, with PRE-AUTHORIZE-then-SETTLE — reserve estimated credits before a job (reject if insufficient), settle against real Replicate per-second cost after, and pad for cold starts (20-60s of billed GPU, ~$0.02-0.08/run on niche models). Flush one aggregated meter event per job, not per model call.

Idempotency + state are non-negotiable: the jobs table in Postgres is the pipeline state machine (status, current_step, progress%, per-step prediction ids, storage keys, idempotency_key). Idempotency keys dedupe re-submitted jobs and replayed webhooks; store Stripe/Replicate event ids to make every webhook handler safe to retry.

Total ops surface for a solo founder is just Vercel + Supabase (both already connected via MCP) + Trigger.dev + Stripe + Replicate — five managed accounts, near-zero servers. Fixed floor ~$55/mo (Vercel Pro $20 + Supabase Pro $25 + Trigger.dev Hobby $10) before variable GPU/storage, with a free-tier path to validate first.

Higgsfield and Pixa MCPs are best used AS model providers/upscalers inside orchestrator steps (e.g., Pixa for the 4K upscale, Higgsfield for generative fills), never inline from a web request — wrap them behind the same async job + state machine so they inherit retries, metering, and progress reporting.

label: Competitive landscape
summary: No one owns single-image to seamless true-4K UE5-native (ORM-packed) PBR with an API. White space = UE-native packing + tiling + honest 4K + API + cheap.

options.name: WithPoly
options.what: Closest rival: free web app, text/image to seamless tileable PBR (full map set, up to 4K/8K).
pros: Full map set, 8K, seamless core, image prompts, free tier
cons: No UE ORM packing/.uasset; no public API; seam quality uneven

options.name: Adobe Substance 3D Sampler 5.x
options.what: Industry-standard image-to-material desktop app; Firefly text/image-to-texture. Quality bar.
pros: Best seamless accuracy; studio-trusted; clean UE import
cons: Desktop+subscription, steep, pricey; no affordable API for a plugin

options.name: Quixel Megascans / Fab
options.what: Photogrammetry PBR LIBRARY (not a generator) in Epic Launcher; the UE-dev reflex.
pros: UE-ready (Bridge, LODs); free in UE; huge library
cons: Library not generation; cannot tile your photo; no upload workflow

options.name: Meshy AI
options.what: Text/image-to-3D-MODEL gen; 4K PBR baked to mesh; strong UE export, real API/MCP.
pros: 4K PBR; FBX/GLB to UE with slots; REST API + MCP
cons: Optimized for objects NOT tiling; baked to mesh; no ORM packing

options.name: Scenario
options.what: Game-art AI with dedicated texture gen: full PBR set, tileable, 8x upscale, custom styles, public API. Most credible pure-texture rival after WithPoly.
pros: Full PBR + seamless + 8K; reference image; public API; style training
cons: Unity-leaning; no UE ORM packing/Datasmith; studio-priced

options.name: Build-on: Ubisoft CHORD, MatLat, Tiled Diffusion
options.what: SOTA open research. Ubisoft Generative Base Material (SIGGRAPH Asia 2025): open seamless-tile -> CHORD single-image-to-SVBRDF -> upscale; weights on GitHub, MatSynth on HF. MatLat (Dec 2025) targets 4K. Tiled Diffusion (CVPR 2025) auto seamless. Self-hostable on Replicate/HF.
options.pricing: Open weights; Replicate/HF GPU cost only.
pros: Open PBR estimation to fine-tune/host (moat); outputs UE-needed maps; fits Replicate+HF
cons: Native 512-1024, 4K upscale error accumulation; weak on metallics; GPLv3 diligence

recommendation: Own the unclaimed slot: ONE photo -> verified-seamless, true-4K, UE5-native PBR in under a minute, for cents, via an API. Five wedges: (1) UE-NATIVE OUTPUT: ORM-packed maps done right (R=AO,G=Roughness,B=Metallic, Linear/Masks, sRGB OFF) + ready UE Material + Datasmith/.uasset; all rivals stop at loose PNGs. (2) TILING SURFACES not models: vs Meshy/Tripo (object-optimized) and converters needing pre-seamless input; Tiled Diffusion/circular-VAE seamlessing + live 3D tiled preview (three.js). (3) HONEST 4K: diffusion is native 512-1024, naive upscale accumulates error; map-aware Real-ESRGAN upscaling, market true-4K-no-mush. (4) API-FIRST: powers the founder NYRA UE plugin (in-editor generate-from-photo) + a B2B line; rivals have no cheap tile/UE-packing API. (5) PRICE: anchors WithPoly 20/mo, Meshy 20-60, Scenario 28-99, Substance 25-60, Megascans free-in-UE; beat with pay-per-texture (cents/4K set on self-hosted compute) + free low-res tier. Moat = self-host/fine-tune Ubisoft CHORD + MatSynth + Tiled-Diffusion on Replicate/HF.
keyTakeaways: No incumbent owns single-photo to seamless to true-4K to UE5-native (ORM-packed) PBR to API. WithPoly/Scenario closest but text-first/Unity-leaning, no UE packing/focused API; Meshy/Tripo model-centric, weak at tiling; Substance/Quixel desktop/library, no cheap API.

UE-readiness is the sharpest wedge: rivals ship loose maps and leave ORM packing (R=AO,G=Rough,B=Metal) and the sRGB-OFF/Linear-Masks gotcha to the user. Correct packing + UE Material/Datasmith/.uasset is concrete value.
True 4K is a documented gap: PBR diffusion is native 512-1024, naive upscaling suffers error accumulation (Ubisoft CHORD, MatLat Dec 2025). Map-aware upscaling = real differentiator.

Build a moat not resell: Ubisoft CHORD/Generative Base Material (SIGGRAPH Asia 2025, GitHub + MatSynth on HF) + Tiled Diffusion (CVPR 2025) self-hosted on Replicate/HF give single-image-to-SVBRDF + AI seamlessing. Caveat: CHORD prototype-grade, weak on metallics.

Pricing anchors: WithPoly 20/mo, Meshy 20-60, Tripo 15.9-111.9, Scenario 28-99, Substance 24.99-59.99; Megascans-in-UE is the zero anchor. Pay-per-texture (cents/4K set) + free low-res tier undercuts while staying bespoke.
API-first is product and distribution: image-in -> UE-ready-ORM-set-out REST endpoint powers the founder NYRA UE5 plugin and is a B2B revenue line no rival plugs into cleanly.
